content.text: An enum is a named set of named constants. TypeScript offers numeric enums, string enums, and const enums — plus the modern alternative of string literal unions that many Angular projects prefer.

attrs.alt: Four enum varieties in TypeScript: numeric enum, string enum, const enum, and string literal union (preferred for Angular)

content.text: A numeric enum assigns integer values starting at 0 to each member. You can override the start value or set any member to a specific integer.

Numeric enums generate a bidirectional object at runtime. This allows mapping from value to name, but it also means any number is assignable to an enum type without a type error — a common source of bugs.

String enums require each member to be explicitly initialised with a string literal. They do not generate reverse mappings and are safer for serialisation — the values you see in logs and APIs are human-readable.
Both numeric and string enums generate a runtime object. For a string enum the emitted code looks like:
Adding the const keyword before enum instructs the compiler to inline every usage at compile time and emit no JavaScript at all. This eliminates the runtime object.
(the default in modern Angular), const enums from external packages cannot be inlined because the compiler processes each file in isolation. Use
only within the same compilation unit.
String Literal Unions — The Modern Alternative

Most Angular style guides and TypeScript documentation recommend string literal unions over enums for new code. They have zero runtime cost, work naturally with JSON, and behave identically to enums for exhaustiveness checking.
Angular templates cannot access TypeScript enums directly unless the enum is exposed as a property of the component class.
Assigning a raw number to a numeric enum variable
TypeScript allows it — runtime value may not match any member
Use string enum or literal union; validate at system boundary
Using const enum across package boundaries
Inlining fails when isolatedModules is enabled
Restrict const enum to the same file or switch to a union
Forgetting to expose enum on the component class for template use
Template can't access the enum — binding throws at runtime
Using enum where JSON is expected
Numeric enum serialises as a number, not a readable string
Use string enum or literal union so serialised values are readable
Not handling all enum members in a switch
Silent runtime bug when a new member is added
TypeScript allows mixing numeric and string members in one enum (called a heterogeneous enum), but this is rarely useful and considered a code smell. Avoid it.
Enum members are either constant (evaluated at compile time) or computed (evaluated at runtime). Computed members require that all subsequent members have explicit initialisers.
When you switch over an enum or union type and add a default branch that calls assertNever, TypeScript catches any newly added member that you have not yet handled.
Extracting Keys and Values from an Enum
Enums vs Literal Unions — Decision Guide
The choice between an enum and a string literal union affects bundle size, JSON serialisation, and how much boilerplate you write. Use this guide to decide:
Values serialised to JSON / REST API
String literal union (values are readable strings)
Continue with string enum; annotate type
Same-file constant set, no runtime object needed
Values come from external config / database
Enums are straightforward to test — use the enum members directly in test assertions. With string literal unions, use the raw strings.
TypeScript offers several ways to define named constant sets:
: auto-incremented integers; generate a runtime bidirectional object; allow unsafe raw-number assignment — avoid in new Angular code
: each member requires an explicit string value; safer than numeric; still generate a runtime object; a raw string literal is not assignable without casting
: inlined at compile time, zero runtime cost; restricted to the same compilation unit when
; zero bundle cost; natural JSON values; work with exhaustive switch; preferred in modern Angular projects

on the component class — or switch to a literal union to avoid the boilerplate entirely
